Interesting. So the error here on `classImplements(Foo, IFoo)` was 
```
InconsistentResolutionOrderError: 
    For object directlyProvides(Foo, IFoo, classImplements(Foo, IFoo)).
```

The underlying cause is the existence of the `foo` object that already had `alsoProvides(foo, IFoo)` called on it. When the declaration on the `Foo` *class* was changed, the declaration (`directlyProvides`) on the `foo` *instance* has to be updated because it depends on the class definition. The C3 algorithm can't find a consistent ordering for the instance.

In this case, another way to solve the problem is to simply discard the instance (`del foo`), or note that the instance no longer implements the interface directly (`noLongerProvides(foo, IFoo)`). In either case, because of the use of weakrefs, it's necessary to run `gc.collect()` once or twice after taking the action.

I like the `noLongerProvides(foo, IFoo)` approach, because we can then continue to use the same `verify_foo()` call on the same instance object, preserving the original intent of the example.

I wonder if there's something we can do in general about this. For example, when we're implicitly updating a `directlyProvides` instance, can we automatically remove interfaces already present in a base (or at least, the `classImplements` base)? This would be the moral equivalent of `noLongerProvides` being called.

> The term `Forward` makes sense if we have another netif and the message is forwarded from it to the OT stack.

There is another netif specified by Thread Spec, which is the Backbone link or adjacent infrastructure link. What I mean is that there is implicitly a Backbone link defined even if there is no `netif` structure defined in OT code for that. 

> But OT stack may be used on SoC type use-case where there is no other network interface other than OT Thread netif itself and then we can use aFromHost to indicate the message is generated/originated by the host application and passed down to OT stack to be sent.

If we check the usage of `aFromNcpHost` throughout the OT code, there is only one usage besides `DUA` code:
- In `ProcessReceiveCallback`, we reject to forward the message back to Host if `aFromNcpHost` is true

However, we already know that a BR **SHOULD** forward the message back to Host if the message was initiated by the Host itself, no matter it's RCP or NCP architecture. 

I think we introduced the `aFromNcpHost` to tell if the message is passed from Host, however that's implementation-specific and not what we actually need. We need to tell if the message is forwarded from another netif, in which case we should never forward the message back to Host.

That said, I think it is valuable to design an API that's compatible with making TCP zero-copy. This will give us the flexibility to implement these zero-copy optimizations in the future. In contrast, if we stick to the simple `otTcpWrite` API, then we won't ever be able to make TCP zero-copy, because copying data is part of that API.

- `plain_text`: no encryption

To write data, you should set the `write` mode:
- `overwrite`: Overwrite existing data with the content of dataframe.
- `append`: Append content of the dataframe to existing data or table.
- `ignore`: Ignore current write operation if data / table already exists without any error.
- `error`: Throw an exception if data or table already exists.
- `errorifexists`: Throw an exception if data or table already exists.

If I reuse your example, in fact from a similar output (146 entries), when I deduplicate the python/stubs files (py/pyi) and I remove the private TypeVar in private modules, the number is in fact 28 entries. It could drop a little more for situation where the private `TypeVar` is used in private functions or private classes, but I have not went that far in the analysis.

It still feels wrong to me to use a private `TypeVar` for the public API for the following reasons.

1. I am certainly influenced by the way of doing things in Java in particular, but I consider these types as part of the signature of the method. In that extent, using private fields (eg. a private inner static class) in the signature of public method is a bad design in Java, as it makes impossible to override this method since you cannot refer to its private components in the signature.
2. Another way to see things is that if we would relate the type annotations with the docstring, we would need to refer to some private elements in the documentation, or find a workaround to express that with only public components.
3. I relate this discussion to another one done on certbot concerning the display test utils, where it was a problem for you to expose some internal components name in the signature of `patch_display_util()`  function if I remember correctly.
4. If we generate the stubs files with `stubgen src` while using a private `TypeVar`, it turns out that the stub file `json_util.pyi` is syntactically wrong because `_T` variable is not generated in the file, while `from_json()`  declares to return `_T`. We will not use the stub files but the implicit type resolution from source code, and I guess mypy is still capable to workaround that. But this shows that the choice of making the `TypeVar` private is not neutral and may create unexpected behaviors.

Also, we need to make sure that we call Create only once for each property even for this use case:
```
MXNET_REGISTER_SUBGRAPH_PROPERTY(default, DefaultSubgraphProperty)
MXNET_REGISTER_SUBGRAPH_PROPERTY(default, DefaultSubgraphProperty)
```
This can be done by adding a map of subgraph property name to the corresponding subgraph property pointer. For this passing the property name along with the create fn is required when calling __REGISTER__. The create gets called and returned subgraph property ptr gets added in __REGISTER__ itself. We can then reuse the already added subgraph property ptr in CreateSubgraphProperty.

> the serialized data is bound to the specific classes and the exact directory structure used when the model is saved. The reason for this is because pickle does not save the model class itself. Rather, it saves a path to the file containing the class

so Model class must be defined somewhere, but mmcv estimator do not have the Model class, so we can not simply use torch.save/load to get the entire model.

And Pytorch recommends to save the model state dict instead of the entire model. so later in mmcv and pytorch estimator, the get_model method will unify to return the model state dict.

The currently version of the code works in a fairly straightforward way in my opinion, in that exactly those things you specified are kept.
So it's legal to: keep an interface and none of its implementations, keep an implementation and not its interface, keep all implementations of an interface but not the interface itself. In the latter two cases, the 'implements X' is just removed from the implementations that are kept.
The user needs to specify unions in order to keep them. If the user doesn't put the union into `types_to_keep`, then the union will be removed, along with all edges to that union.
Putting random strings or scalar/enum/query type in `types_to_keep` doesn't do anything. It's a little like how the input dictionary into `merge_schemas` works, random strings/scalars/other things that don't get used are just ignored.

> This interface would be impossible to use for a caller who is managing side storage for large values themselves (which is probably the best approach

I agree that something needs to change, but I don't think it's this.  When you insert data, the algorithm should be:

```
1. Get the MARF hash of the value
2. Store the key/value-hash in the MARF
3. Store the value and value-hash in the data store
```

To read data, the algorithm is:
```
1. Given the chain tip and key, get the MARF value hash
2. Given the value hash, get the associated value
3. (optional) Verify the value hashes to the value hash
```

So, the MARF interface would be:

```rust
fn get_value_hash(value: &String) -> MARFValue;
fn insert(&mut self, key: &String, value_hash: &MARFValue) -> Result<(), Error>;
fn get(&mut self, block_hash: &BlockHeaderHash, key: &String) -> Result<Option<MARFValue>, Error>
```

And, the interface for `MARFValue` would be:

```rust
fn from_value(s: &String) -> MARFValue;
fn verify(&self, s: &String) -> bool;
```

I think there are a few cases to consider (when no rules are put into place):

1. A customer generating < 100 traces per second per tracer.
  a. They will have everything sampled as a p1, no issues
  b. It also doesn't matter how many services they are reporting per tracer since they are under the limit anyways
2. A customer generating > 100 traces per second per tracer, but only 1 top level service per tracer
  a. It won't necessarily be "uniform" in the correct sense since it'll just mark everything over 100 traces per second a p0
  b. Signature sampling in the agent should pick up on some interesting p0s being sent
3. A customer with multiple top level services per tracer sending > 100 traces per second
  a. This case isn't ideal since we do not get uniform sampling across services with regards to the 100 traces per second limit
  b. If Service A generates 100 traces, and then Service B generates 100 all within a second, then Service B will never have any traces sampled, no bueno
  c. Signature sampling in the agent should pick up a few interesting p0 traces
4. (Are there others I forgot? These seem to be the most common cases I can think of)

I think condition 1 is likely the most common across all of our customers, and most people are probably fine (with the new 100 traces per second per tracer limit in place).

I think conditions 2 and 3 are mostly the cases with our larger customers, condition 2 is probably more common than condition 3.

For condition 2, we give people the ability with this new sampler to limit how many traces are generated for certain resources so they have the control to balance their traces amongst the 100 traces per second.

For condition 3, we give people the ability with this new sampler to limit how many traces are generates for a certain service so they have the control to balance their traces amongst the 100 traces per second.

(also everyone has the ability to bump the 100 traces per second up to a higher number as well).

While we can do better to not force people to manually add rules to control their sampling, the benefits of this new sampler is to be easy to understand and easy to control on your own.

The size of `addresses` is `kIPv6AddressesNumMax`. 
We also have this check to make sure that the number of addresses in the message never exceed `kIPv6AddressesNumMax`. So accesses to `addresses` are always valid. 
```c++
    VerifyOrExit(addressesLength / sizeof(Ip6::Address) <= kIPv6AddressesNumMax,
                 status = ThreadStatusTlv::kMlrGeneralFailure);
```

Each address read from message is either `success` or `failed`. So `failedAddressNum + successAddressNum` won't exceed `kIPv6AddressesNumMax` either. So, the two indexes won't reach each other in any case.

> And the most important, I'm tired of merging dependabot's PR almost every day once in a while.

Dependabot will still generate PRs that update the lock file, that PR just won't include an update to pyproject.toml. 

The pyproject.toml is more about what dependencies can be used that still result in a runnable version of our package. So if furo is listed as wildcard, anyone with any version of furo installed will not have that version updated (unless they also use our lock file, with the exact version).

For this reason, I like to specify a minimum version for dependencies, to make sure that no one will end up using some really old version, so, even with furo, I'd image the first ever released version probably wouldn't be compatible with the current one anymore.

However since furo doesn't use semvers, instead of using the usual caret requirement syntax, we could use the inequality one, like say `furo>=2022-08-16`, to at least have that minimum version.
Also `getSanitizedModulePath` might be a more clear name, as our go dependency replacement only works in terms of modules (specifying a package won't work).

So now that I am thinking about it, generated files should not be stored in the libraries path any how. They should be stored in the users application data or in the program data. This would be %appdata% (c:\users\{user name}\appdata\roaming) or %programdata% (c:\programdata). The generated modules do not need to be located in the comtypes.gen folder. the path to where the generated files actually exist needs to be set in comtypes.gen.\_\_path\_\_. This allows comtypes to function properly.

There can also potentially be another issue with writing the files to a folder in the library path.
The users have the ability to install Python into the program files directory. The thing is that with Windows 10 there can be permissions issues with an application creating files in the program files directory or any of it's sub folders.  So writing the generated files to either program data or app data is going to kill 2 birds with a single stone. It will solve the zip_safe issue and it will also solve a potential permissions problem on Windows 10.

Also if we need 100 pages on a query that looks like `A -> B`, and the ideal pagination location is `A` but it can support only 10 pages, we may want to produce a plan like "10 pages at `A`, and for each of them, 10 pages at `B`". While this is not the ideal plan, it's better than not paginating at all, and arguably it's better (up to a point) than only producing 10 pages (the ones at `A`). Where it ceases to be better than only producing 10 pages at `A` is if we actually needed say 10000 pages total, and we needed to split `B` into so many pieces that we suspect our pages might be severely unbalanced due to `A-B` correlations, but that scenario ends badly no matter what we do.

Obviously, if we are ever in this situation, the correct long-term answer is to collect more stats at `A`. This discussion is only about the fall-back scenario if we haven't yet collected all the stats we want.

@marc-hb @mbolivar-nordic great tips, I really appreciate it. I added `--recursive` option as a default one, because for now it seems to be the best option and I also added test case for that in one of tests.

> It does leave behind any commits that aren't part of the new manifest-rev unless you use -r. It leaves a detached HEAD unless you use -r or sometimes when you use -k.

I agree with @marc-hb that "west with submodules" should try to at least keep the default behavior:

    leave a detached HEAD checked out
    leave any uncommitted working tree changes alone
    error out if uncommited changes conflict with the new submodule revision

Is that possible to do with git submodules?
>

Actually current behavior of submodules with `--checkout` option is almost the same, as described. I wrote almost, because it leaves at detached HEAD state, leaves uncommitted changes alone, prints error in case of conflicts and overrides new commits which are not part of new manifest-rev, but it doesn't care about `-r` or `-k` option. I tried to fix it using `--rebase` and `--checkout` options, but few problems occurred:

- Current west update strategy assumes that we should make rebase or keep descendants only when project is not in the detached state. That implies problem when some changes are done in the project's submodule and it should be rebased, but project itself is not modified, so rebase will not be selected. That's not a big problem at all, because new method introducing exact same behavior for submodules could be implemented to solve it.

- Real problem is using `--recursive` option, because it implies that all submodules of submodule will be updated the same way, as submodule is (with `--rebase`, `--checkout` or anything else). That means that when using `-r` submodules on the first level will behave same way as projects (so rebase when modified or checkout otherwise) and on other levels will always do the same as parent submodule did, without possibility to select other strategy. This will raise confusion mentioned by @marc-hb.  

What are your opinions on that? Do you think making assumption that submodules are always checked out and don't support rebase is acceptable? Summing up - this way default `west update` should work properly, but command extensions unfortunately not.

Ok, so I thought about `ChainContext` for a bit longer than I care to admit. But I think I found a good direction:

If you look at where the properties derived from `context` are used, it's mostly in `ETHProtocol`. That's actually great, and makes a lot of sense: these protocol specific values are only used where we know they should have been set, inside `ETHProtocol` (as opposed to the base peer kind of knowing about them, but not really because it's this opaque type-busting `context` thing).

So the idea is `ETHProtocol` should actually be the thing that receives these protocol specific connection data on init, as part of the peer factory, which would set curried versions of ETHProtocol on `ETHPeer`s `_supported_sub_protocols`. If you follow that rabbit hole, I think that means you want to break the dependency that protocol has on peer. The subprotocols would expose one unifying API to the peer: `def encode_request(request: BaseRequest) -> (header: bytes, body: bytes)`. Then the peer would use that to expose `peer.send_request(request)`, which uses the protocol encoding under the hood.

So it's potentially a big-ish change. Also, my thoughts/explanation is probably too messy to really convey what I'm thinking. Luckily, I think most of this PR wouldn't change (or at least, not any more than the pre-PR code would change). So although I want to keep exploring the change, I don't think it needs to hold up the PR. Probably the easiest way forward would be to merge this PR and then for me to make a rough WIP version of what I'm thinking, for us to talk about.

> If a subscription is a tenant level account, does it mean it only belong to one tenant? 

In that case, the account **is** a tenant with no subscriptions, not "belong to". The naming of `subscription_dict` is inaccurate. It contains both subscription and tenant accounts. 

> In this case, the rest api response does not has homeTenentId? 

This tenant account is not generated from REST, but manually built up:

https://github.com/Azure/azure-cli/blob/944c0c3b1f30530a280bfaa6b7c7d799e3248f28/src/azure-cli-core/azure/cli/core/_profile.py#L270-L279

> Why do we need this check here?

It doesn't make sense to include `home_tenant_id` and `managed_by_tenants` (whichs are subscription account's attributes) for a tenant account.

In fact, this is all due to the faulty original design that mixes subscription and tenant in the same list. This will and must be changed in the future to reflect the hierarchy. Tenant is a one-level-higher concept than subscription.

I'm not sure we want to be setting the exact backoff parameters on behalf of our users -- everyone has different use cases and we as a library should not be enforcing our assumptions on applications.

We probably want to expose the consistently useful bits of this (eg. > how to mock the value of `MD5` and the `hash_dict` being passed into `on_disk_cache`

IIUC, once the tar file is created, then you can compute the hash value of the tar, and patch the value of `torchtext.datasets.amazonreviewpolarity.MD5`. 
Mocking the `on_disk_cache` is an alternative way to do so, but IMO, it's less flexible, because it requires precise understanding of how and when the function is called, which can be changed anytime in the future. 

Something like the following should work.  the patch command in `setUpClass` then undo the patch in `tearDownClass`.
https://docs.python.org/3/library/unittest.mock.html#patch-methods-start-and-stop

```python
def setUpClass(cls):
    super().setUpClass()
    cls.root_dir = cls.get_base_temp_dir()
    cls.samples = get_mock_dataset(cls.root_dir)
    val = compute_hash(<the archive file>)
    cls.patcher = patch('torchtext.datasets.amazonreviewpolarity.MD5', val)
    cls.patcher.start()

def tearDownClass(cls):
    super().tearDownClass()
    cls.patcher.stop()
```

https://github.com/py-moneyed/py-moneyed/blob/c495642244f629e4ed330b25438edbd517175146/src/moneyed/classes.py#L193-L197

`None` would get cast to `"NONE"` before it's validated as a currency code.

In addition to that, if we look at the `__init__` signature of `class Money` in `py-moneyed` (that runs the linked code above) it doesn't define any support for passing `currency=None`;

https://github.com/py-moneyed/py-moneyed/blob/c495642244f629e4ed330b25438edbd517175146/src/moneyed/classes.py#L173-L179

As such, I'm leaning towards the interpretation that `None` _is already an_ unsupported value for a currency code, therefore resulting in undefined behaviour (although kindly enough with the version of `py-moneyed` that's used we get an error with `"NONE"` attempted as currency code).

Right, an assert may be appropriate in such a situation where you have a condition that should theoretically *never* fail. Note that it will be stripped away with "-O" which can be seen as an advantage (better performance in production) or disadvantage ("impossible" but still happening errors in production not detected). If you use asserts, then you should do it consistently for such cases in the whole codebase, (see also [here](https://github.com/graphql-python/graphql-core-legacy/issues/244)).

One of the reasons to add an "if isinstance" or an "assert" is that mypy should (in theory) understand that so that you don't need the "cast" (not sure if that works in the latest version of mypy). It is also a hint to the reader of the code. Btw, GraphQL-Core also has an `assert_scalar_type` function that you can use here. It raises a TypeError though instead of an AssertionError. Its return value is GraphQLScalarType so that should also help mypy.

I also add a "# pragma: no cover" to not decrease coverage for such code that should in theory never execute.

@rretanubun I do not think  the value of `icmp6Header` pointer matters in this situation.
I think `sizeof` only cares about the type of its expersion. So this is equivalent to `sizeof(Ip6::Icmp::Header)`.

_From [sizeof operator (cppreference)](https://en.cppreference.com/w/cpp/language/sizeof):
When applied to an expression, sizeof does not evaluate the expression, and even if the expression designates a polymorphic object, the result is the size of the static type of the expression_

That said, it is possible that some code checker tools do not like the fact that `*icmp6Header` is being used without variable being set first. I suggest to use direct type in this case, i.e.
```
VerifyOrExit(aBuffer != nullptr && aBufferLength >= sizeof(Ip6::Icmp::Header), error = kErrorParse);
```

To put it another way, there is now a risk that `skip_row()` won't be called.  I see that this could break code that has been around for a while.

I had a look at the PRs related to this code ([here](https://github.com/django-import-export/django-import-export/commit/517f10199f125f3c0387ac1d0417a9a554e17e10) and [here](https://github.com/django-import-export/django-import-export/pull/852/files)) to see if this it was deliberate choice to ignore validation errors for `skip_row()`.  I don't think it was a deliberate choice and I think it is a valid bug.

However, since we are changing `skip_row()` in release 3, so we could pass `validation_errors` in and the implementer can decide whether they want to handle the errors or not.  For example:

```
def skip_row(self, instance, original, row, validation_errors=None):
  if validation_errors:
    return False
```

This means that if there are validation errors, then they will be reported correctly.  This is probably the safest approach (and anyone who wants to ignore validation errors can do so).

Again, interesting discussion, one last try. I'd like to share the different between "stream" and "streaming" from the word meaning perspective so far I understood. "Streaming" is a kind of macro virtual concept, it means everything about the stream processing, that is why it is fine to call the module ray streaming at the concept level. There is no "a" streaming, and we cannot define the runtime context of "a" streaming. On the other hand, "stream" is a concrete object, each DAG we build (based on data streams, stream operators, etc.), deploy, and run, is "a" stream. It is nature to say each running stream has a runtime context, therefore we got the name StreamRuntimeContext which means the runtime context of a stream.

I agree that it's not a great solution, I rewrote it to simply remove all special tokens and add special tokens back in afterwards.

I think that in reality we won't ever get such sequences in `indices_to_tokens`. To my understanding max length is there to fold sequences that are too long for the given transformer into shorter segments. I think currently the only use case of max length is for the `PretrainedTransformerEmbedder`, which takes care of unfolding itself (besides that, those are embeddings and not ids anyways).

I am not sure yet how we should deal with models that BART that generate variable length output. What if we feed in a very long article that is split up into several segments and then text is generated for each segment? Each segment is missing context from other segments, so we can't expect the text generated from each segment to make sense, especially not if we would simply concatenate them together.

Anyways, the reason I included that code is to make `indices_to_tokens` a proper inverse
of `tokens_to_indices`. Otherwise, if you use `tokens_to_indices` with `max_length` and then use `indices_to_tokens`, you will get different output due to the special tokens.

We might want to consider `action="append"` here instead, and a singular option that can repeat. Like `--node_type t2.large:1.5 --node_type c5.4xlarge/c5a.4xlarge:0.42`.

Either way we have to make sure to fix the docs/examples to show it the way that actually works on the command line, and not with square brackets.

> Do we need that "+ 1"? I'm struggling to understand if that's needed and why, maybe the "<=" is enough?

Yes, we do need it:
- `numsync` is a numeric value from `synchronous_standby_names` and doesn't include the leader.
- `quorum` is a numeric value from the `/sync` key and it's meaning is - how many other nodes we need to see in order to make a decision. It's value is calculated as `len(sync) - numsync`.

Lets say out `a` is the leader name and we have `ANY 1 (b,c)` in `synchronous_standby_names`.
In the /sync key we will have: `{leader: 'a', sync: 'b,c' , quorum: 1}`

If we start performing this check we will get:
- `voters = {a,b,c}`
- `sync = {a,b,c}`
- `len(sync | voters) = 3`
- `quorum + numsync = 2`

That is, even for a normal (stable) configuration we need both, `+1` and `<=`.

When we change the topology (adding/removing/replacing nodes or changing `numsync`/`quorum` values) the rule also must be followed.

The answer is (2).  In verifying a back-pointer from block _B_ and node _N_ to block _B - K_ to node _N'_, it is sufficient to:

* prove that block _B - K_'s root hash is, in fact, _K_ blocks prior to _B_ (using a shunt proof)
* prove that node _N'_ is a child of the root of _B - K_, and is located in a path that is prefixed with the path to node _N_ from _B_ (using a segment proof).

> As a further test of my understanding, the reason for doing this is to avoid copying the node hash in addition to the blockhash (which is necessary for lookups in the fork table in any case)?

That is correct.  The fork table can fit entirely in RAM for the foreseeable future, so inserting the hash of block _B - K_ as the hash of _N'_ when calculating the root hash of _B_ does not carry any I/O penalty.  The naive way to go about this is to simply load the hash of _N'_'s children in _B - K_, but this would take no fewer than _C+1_ disk seeks for _C_ children of _N'_ (at least 1 seek is required to load _N'_'s data).

Let's say before line `Send():261`, `mTxHead > 0`, `mTxLength == sizeof(mTxBuffer)`, after `Send()`, `mSendLength == kTxBufferSize - mTxHead`. 

After `otPlatUartFlush():268`, `kTxBufferSize - mTxHead` bytes are sent, but `mTxHead` bytes are left.

After `SendDoneTask():273`, `mSendLength == 0`, but `mTxLength == mTxHead`, and the left bytes are delivered to platform, but not actually sent.

Then more bytes are filled and it goes to `Send():261` again, however, this time, since `mSendLength` is not `0`, it directly returns and only `mTxHead` bytes get flushed by `otPlatUartFlush():268`. Those newly appended byte are not sent.

This code take advantage of the simulation platform to run more consistency  checks (with higher overhead).
I believe it is a good practice:
- It allows us to catch bugs more quickly, and gives us much more information when bugs are found. Unittests do cover the basic usage but might unable to cover some corner cases, or it might not be updated properly in future code refactoring. 
- It does not introduce any overhead on any platform except simulation, on which platform we don't care about performance much.

##### For `std::is_heap`
I used `std::is_heap` to avoid implementing it manually. We upgraded to C++11 standard recently so that we can take advantage of modern C++ features. Maybe we can have a further discussion on which std libraries can we use and which we can't.

No, the problem is that if you send an interrupt it's not seen by the shell that has the trap. It doesn't work with

```
trap copy_output SIGTERM SIGINT EXIT
runuser ...
```
or
```
trap copy_output SIGTERM
runuser ...
copy_output
```

Just
```
trap copy_output SIGTERM
runuser ... &
wait $!
copy_output
```

If it has this, it runs the copy twice on a successful run.
```
trap copy_output SIGTERM EXIT
runuser ... &
wait $?
copy_output
```

Finally, we need the sigterm trap in case the container is shutdown with `docker stop` or `docker kill`.

`forward` variable is used to indicate whether the message should be forwarded or not and not whether the message was actually forwarded. 

There are other checks before and after this part of the code where we can exit (e.g., we have the `if (!ShouldForwardToThread(messageInfo))` check before and the hop limit check after) and in both cases the message won't be forwarded but the `forward` variable remains `true` as before.

If we want to change the meaning of `forward` for possible future use, we would need to change all other instances as well. I would say it's better not to add additional code that serves no immediate purpose or use. 

The reason for the existing code setting `forward` to `false` here seems to be to ensure message is freed.

If in future there is a need to determine whether the message was successfully forwarded, the best way to do it, would be to add it right after the `SuccessOrExit(error = Get<ThreadNetif>().SendMessage(aMessage))` statement.

The "warning" header and its contents are completely custom items that happen to be added by CacheControl. They are of no functional use to the client, they don't prove that the response will actually be cached as desired, and testing them means that if ever anyone swaps out CacheControl for a different module, or possibly if ever CacheControl is upgraded, the test will fail.

What I envisaged here was to make multiple `session.get` calls to the same URL, and check the response was returned from the cache or not, as expected. You could use the `from_cache` property, but even that is a little bit testing internals. Even better would be to update the content returned by the endpoint. Thx for pointer on executors. My preferred solution here relates to my point above about graphs.

In several places I've seen collections of ops described as jobs, or references to connecting ops to form a job. For instance, the headlines for Op and Job concept pages in current docs say:

- "Multiple ops can be connected to create a Job."
- "A job is an executable graph of ops"

I understand a decision was made to emphasize jobs over graphs at some point, presumably because it introduces fewer abstractions when you're trying to explain how to get something done.

But I think this has resulted in elisions that are ultimately costly to the user, because it creates confusion about Dagster works. IMO it is better to always describe collections of connected ops  as capital-g "Graphs", and emphasize that a Graph is a component of a Job-- Ops are computational units, they can be wired together into Graphs, and a Job binds configuration/resources and an executor (and maybe some other stuff) with a Graph. Use of the @job decorator is just a way of creating an anonymous Graph, analogous to an inline lambda in Python.

Following this approach, I'd edit that last sentence like so:

>Collections of ops (i.e. [Graphs](/concepts/ops-jobs-graphs/jobs-graphs)) are bound via [Jobs](/concepts/ops-jobs-graphs/jobs-graphs) to an appropriate [Executor](/deployment/executors) for single-process execution or distribution across a cluster.

My heuristic for exceptions vs returning none is to get a sense for which is the 'exceptional' circumstance. I would say that *not* finding either a netrc file or an entry for a host is the most common circumstance, even when trust_env is true, while finding an entry is the exceptional circumstance. This is why I prefer it returning None vs exception. There is also a performance penalty here, that is paid for every request when `trust_env` is true. https://switowski.com/blog/ask-for-permission-or-look-before-you-leap/ quantifies this a little, and also suggests the same heuristic I am suggesting - both conceptually and from a performance perspective.

Python 3.11 makes this a bit better with zero cost exceptions (https://bugs.python.org/issue40222) but that only applies if an exception is not actually raised. In this case, as an exception will be raised most of the time so we do not benefit from that optimization.

So my suggestion is to leave this as is. However, given y'all are the maintainers, I will happily fold and implement the suggested changes if you think that is the better way to go :)

TL; DR - Not holding the lock after changing the state to IN_PROGRESS indeed seems safe. However, I would still like to see the effect of this change covered by a test before merging. I can help with this, if needed.

---
**Long reply:**

I checked the Batch code, and from what I can tell, that the following threads are relevant:
- `MonitorBatchPublisher` (if autcommit == True): Triggers `_commit()` after `max_latency` time elapses.
- `CommitBatchPublisher`: If somebody calls `commit()` and the batch is in ACCEPTING_MESSAGES state, `_commit()` gets called in this thread.
- User thread(s): When calling `publisher.publish()`, `batch.publish()` gets called. If that causes an overflow, `commit()` is called in the `CommitBatchPublisher` thread.

The first thing that `_commit()` does after acquiring the lock is changing the batch status to IN_PROGRESS.

In that state, any further calls to `publish()` [become a no-op](https://github.com/googleapis/google-cloud-python/blob/master/pubsub/google/cloud/pubsub_v1/publisher/_batch/thread.py#L280-L282), because the `will_accept()` check [returns `False`](https://github.com/googleapis/google-cloud-python/blob/master/pubsub/google/cloud/pubsub_v1/publisher/_batch/base.py#L123-L124) for any state that is not ACCEPTING_MESSAGES.

Additionally, any other `_commit()` calls also [become a no-op](https://github.com/googleapis/google-cloud-python/blob/master/pubsub/google/cloud/pubsub_v1/publisher/_batch/thread.py#L181-L188), because the batch status is now IN_PROGRESS.

Only the thread that changed the state to IN_PROGRESS can proceed, thus shielding the rest of the `_commit()` method with `_state_lock` indeed seems unnecessary, which includes the call to `self._client.api.publish()`.

_Without_ the `_state_lock` held, further calls to `batch.publish()` will not block longer than needed. The batch will simply not accept a new message, return `False`, and `publisher.publish() `will create an entirely [new batch](https://github.com/googleapis/google-cloud-python/blob/master/pubsub/google/cloud/pubsub_v1/publisher/client.py#L236-L238) as a result.

FWIW, our cancellable states are:
* `claim_work`: When cancelled, we jump to `except CancelledError` and return
* `sleep`: When cancelled, we jump to `except CancelledError` and return
* `verify_chain_of_trust`: When cancelled, skip running the task itself. Upload artifacts to Taskcluster and inform of `worker-shutdown` status (note that the `CancelledError` is caught in `do_run_task`!)
* Task process (within `run_task`): When cancelled, kill the process. Upload artifacts to Taskcluster and inform of `worker-shutdown` status (note that `CancelledError` is not raised! The process is killed, which triggers the follow-up behaviour)

I agree that perhaps the post-task behaviour should perhaps be different if the task was cancelled partially through a build - perhaps just: {upload `cot.log`, upload `live_backing.log`, set task status}, yeah. Should that be handled here, or in follow-up? I'm flexible :)

Two of the lp tests (lp_3 and lp_4 in cvxpy/tests/solver_test_helpers.py) were passed and have bounds within the constraints similar to:

`# Infeasible
x = cp.Variable((10, 1))
objective = cp.Minimize(cp.sum(x))
prob = cp.Problem(objective, [x >= 1, x <= 0])
prob.solve(verbose=True, solver="SCIPY")`

As far as I can tell, as long as the bounds for CVXPY are contained within the constraints of the Problem instance, they will be passed to the solver through the constraints. With the "bounds" parameter within the scipy_options dictionary the user has the ability to specify exactly the same bounds:

`# Infeasible
x = cp.Variable((10, 1))
objective = cp.Minimize(cp.sum(x))
prob = cp.Problem(objective)
prob.solve(verbose=True, solver="SCIPY", scipy_options={'bounds':(1, 0)})`

From what I can see, the only downside of keeping the bounds parameter, is that if the user were to try another solver and only specified the bounds through that parameter, then the other solver would not see those bounds. But if the user has specified the bounds using the first example above, then everything should work as normal. 

Should I still go ahead and raise an error if the user specifies "bounds" within scipy_options to force the user to include them within the constraints of the CVXPY Problem instance?

As for the loop: no.  Restarting the read will also restart timeout and by this change the timing.  I'm using pyserial for pymodbus and the read has to return within 3.5 character's time to distinguish between two packets.

Any serial application should be able to cope with a read returning zero bytes.  But what I did for pyserial 3.3 was to catch EAGAIN plus EINTR and ignore them (returning zero bytes immediately) to not have to handle that in the aplication.  Those exceptions will be delivered by the kernel when waiting is interrupted, which in case of a Modbus communication with a high probability would be either before the packet (same as overall timeout expired) or after it when VTIME is used to detect the end of the packet.

But the current state of VTIMESerial.read did not handle exceptions at all, so I was reluctant to add that.  And as I just detected: in 3.3 I've changed class Serial and not VTIMESerial.  I'm not to fond of two classes, why does VTIMESerial exist?

Please don't use a center-based representation.  That way lies the madness of ADQL's ill-fated BOX.

A true range-based representation is fundamentally not relocatable in latitude - its shape is inherently different for different latitudes.

A geometrically natural center-based representation needs to be fully relocatable - it should have the same shape anywhere on the sphere as long as its size parameters stay the same.  (It's a coordinate-free geometrical concept.)  

But it's *so* likely to be confused with the coordinate-range region that people have a lot of trouble with it.  BOX is being proposed for withdrawal from ADQL because it caused so much trouble of this kind.

> > when could it happen that `IsRunning() && !mAutoStartDidSelectServer` can be satisfied.
> 
> You mention one way, but the more common way is that auto-start is enabled by default (which is the feature we support through an OT config) and then explicit call to `Start()` client with a specific server.

Do you mean a explicit call to `Start()` with one server chosen from the Network Data, meaning the user want to implement custom rules for choosing a server?

> We can add more conditions/restrictions that for example require user to disable auto-start mode before calling `Start()` and/or not allow user to change auto-start mode after `Start()` but there are some downsides to this:
> 
> * We need to add more code to check for these combinations and return error to user (increase code size for not so common behaviors).

Not necessarily to increase code size? Currently we have code logic checking whether the server is actually chosen by `auto-start` feature even when `auto-start` feature is ON. These logic won't be necessary in the proposed strategy. 

> * It makes the documentation/description of the APIs (the API use policy) more complex.
> * It's true that user can use the APIs in uncommon ways (like enable `auto-start` after calling `Start()` which is pointless) but the way I see it is that as long as we behave consistently and correctly, we don't need to explicitly protect user from using uncommon API combinations.

In this current strategy, enabling `auto-start` after calling `Start()` would return OK. I think this might be a little bit confusing to users. It seems that `auto-start` is successfully, but the client is still **NOT** using servers in Network Data.

> For longer-term, we may add use-cases where we expand the auto-start feature and want to use/enable it even after the client is started by `Start()` call.
> 
> * A possible future enehancment is to use auto-start mode to (also) help switch the SRP server if we fail to get a response from the currently selected SRP server after many attempts/retries.
>   
>   * In the current implementation when we select a server, we stick with it even if we never receive a response from it. We switch the server when the SRP server entry/info is no longer seen in Thread network-data.
>   * Basically the idea of this enhancement is that if we don't get a response from the current SRP server (after many retries), we try to see if there is any other SRP server options (from network-data) and switch to it.
> * We may want to use/enable this behavior for both cases where the SRP server is selected by auto-start or through `Start()` call.

I think this is actually a good point. I think the `auto-start` feature can simply be a automatic way of adding servers, but the strategy of selecting server is independent on whether the servers are input from Network Data or user configuration. User would also be able to add multiple servers for the SRP client to chose the best server. 

That being said, I am fine that we work out a better solution in the future.

Didn't we have 32 and 64 bit C api functions?

It sounds counter intuitive initially, but if we have overloaded c api functions we could always expose them. If we're compiled without 64bit Support, the c api would then return an error instead of passing on the request. IMO this design makes the typing a bit more explicit and transparent (while I have to admit that I generally dislike the verbose explicity in c++) instead of hiding all the magic in a simple typedef override and some preprocessor statements.

The logic in the frontend would then be to call the 32bit c api functions with small tensors and 64bit one with large tensors. Of course it needs some tweaking because that's not a fail proof design against overflow of the intermediary results.

What it seems like to me is that a C Api is supposed to be a static contract that is always present. But with the 64bit Support we're now introducing versions of the c api that depend on the compile options. This then breaks the exchangability and decoupling. In the end it's a matter of taste, but I think we should uphold ourselves to a standard where the c api never changes but the backend might behave differently depending on the chosen options. At the same time, we might want to make it a rule that c api function definitions must not be conditional. 

It's a bit derailing, but what do you think about that idea?

 - When source matched: `RX_FRM_ACCEPTED -> SRC_MATCH_FOUND -> RXPKTDONE`
 - When source not matched: `RX_FRM_ACCEPTED -> RXPKTDONE`

So, it is guaranteed that there will always be a `RXPKTDONE ` after `SRC_MATCH_FOUND`. 
For messages filtered by frame filtering, none of `RX_FRM_ACCEPTED -> SRC_MATCH_FOUND -> RXPKTDONE` will be triggered.

```suggestion
                logger.info("INFO: Skipping fixing symlink, it already exists.")
            else:
                drive = pathlib.PureWindowsPath(path.drive)
                local_directory = drive.joinpath(pathlib.PurePosixPath(output))
                path.unlink()
                path.symlink_to(local_directory, target_is_directory=True)
```

Yes and yes. `numpy` testing functions coerce everything into an `np.ndarray` first and than compare. We consciously opted against that for mainly two reasons:

1. If you have tests parametrized over operators like PyTorch core does, it is impossible to see if the returned type is correct. For example

```py
>>> expected = 1.0
>>> np.testing.assert_allclose(np.array(expected), expected)
>>> torch.testing.assert_close(torch.tensor(expected), expected)
TypeError: No comparison pair was able to handle inputs of type <class 'torch.Tensor'> and <class 'float'>.
```

2. It allows us to compare sequences of tensors with different shapes elementwise

    ```py
    >>> shapes = [(2, 3), (2, 4)]
    >>> np.testing.assert_allclose(*([np.ones(shape) for shape in shapes],) * 2)
    ValueError: could not broadcast input array from shape (2,3) into shape (2,)
    >>> torch.testing.assert_close(*([torch.ones(shape) for shape in shapes],) * 2)
    ```

     This aligns nicely with the ability of `torch.testing.assert_close` to compare mappings elementwise, which is not possible with the `numpy` functions.

And in the conda test, do you think you could add a test to make sure the GC works when we specify conda in a task?  (It should work, right? because the references will be counted per worker.). Specifically, I'm interested in the following situation:
1. Start a task with conda env A
2. Start an actor with conda env A
3. Kill the actor (and perhaps wait for some time)
4. Start another task with conda env A

With the naive per-actor ref counting, the refcount would go to 0 at step 3, so the conda env would be deleted and step 4 would fail with ModuleNotFoundError (because the second task would run on the same worker as the first task, but the conda environment is gone.)

But with this PR, it should work properly because the worker will contribute to the refcount.

The default value for the probability of skimage is set to 1. Therefore, the existing skimage code always returns the infinite number of trials. I think this is an inefficient way.

The problem could occur like this, as an example:
- user sends in data in range [0,1] for downsizing, and sets `anti_aliasing=True`, `order=0` and `clip=True`, expecting output to be also in range [0,1]
- data is first filtered with `ndi.gaussian_filter`, which can produce out of range data
- the actual resize operation is performed
- `_clip_warp_output` is called and does nothing since `order=0`
- the user get output with out of range data caused by the first step, while `clip=True`... My opinion is that is unexpected behavior.

That's why I propose that `_clip_warp_output` knows about `anti_aliasing`, to clip out of range data potentially caused by anti_aliasing operation ! :smiley:

This was probably the trickiest one to get right. I believe what the original code was doing is essentially a fold / aggregate (although the empty case was returning `MkTrue()`, which isn't the initial value for Xor, but I've preserved that behaviour here).

The trickiness with the disposing is that we only want to dispose of the temporary objects created as part of the fold. To ensure this is the case I seeded `Aggregate` with `MkFalse` so that in the first call to the lambda it will dispose of this temp object once the `MkXor` has been called. Each subsequent call to the lambda will dispose of `MkXor` created in the previous invocation. Therefore only objects explicitly created in this method (the intermediate values of the fold) are passed to the `using` statement. The final value is not disposed of as it is passed back to the caller and therefore they own it.

In the trivial case of an empty list therefore is nothing to dispose because we just return `MkTrue()`.

> It's worth considering whether we really want to do this. IIRC, during the design discussion there seemed to be consensus that if you ran ./pants test '**/*' it was ok to run "test per file"... ie, for subtarget addresses to be used. Perhaps there have been other discoveries since then that suggest that that would not be a good idea, but it would seem to be much easier, and possibly more consistent?

I don't follow. We would end up running "test per file", except if there were multiple owners for that file. This test is only what to do if you run `./pants test '**/*' ::`. In that case, the `::` would "win out" over the generated subtargets from the file args. This is simply a test for the behavior added in https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/pull/10345.

> I know that we're trying to defer the "multiple owning targets" syntax, but if it avoids this type of special casing, it might be worth it.

I disagree on it being a priority for Pants 2.0. The special casing is complex, but it's well isolated to `internals/graph.py` and it's well tested. If it were leaking throughout the codebase, I would think differently.

In terms of special-casing being complex to users, I think we teach this that "When it's safe to do so, Pants will try to use a more precise target than you put in your BUILD file. Otherwise, it will use the coarser target you explicitly defined."

This behavior is different because

1) the pool size is limited so it doesn't matter how many items we have in our list
2) we want more granularity in case loading a history fails. If we have 1000 days, and twitterscraper fails to load a day 10% of the time, we'll run twitterscraper for a single day 1,100 times on average. If our interval is one month instead of one day, we'll succeed retrieving a month 4% (90%^30) of the time, resulting in 20,000 work units instead of 1,100.
3) We save precious memory by only going one day at the time. Emulated browsers store increasingly more state as we scroll further.

I'd argue we don't rely on this environment variables anymore. They were needed before, when `__conda_exe()` and friends were defined like this:

```bash
$CONDA_EXE $_CE_M $_CE_CONDA $@
```

In normal circumstances, these variables were defined as:

* `CONDA_EXE`: path to `$BASE_PREFIX/bin/conda` (Unix) or `$BASE_PREFIX/Scripts/conda.exe` (Windows) -- in other words, the Python entry points.
* `_CE_M` and `_CE_CONDA`: empty string

In `dev` mode, they would change to
* `CONDA_EXE`: path to `python` executable in the base environment
* `_CE_M`: `-m`
* `_CE_CONDA`: `conda`

With this hack, we could do `conda.exe install ...` in normal mode and `python -m conda install ...`  in dev mode. However, since we are now ALWAYS using `python -m conda`, it makes no sense to provide this option. If anything, it can create issues because the user can accidentally delete or redefine `_CE_M` and `_CE_CONDA` and break the integration.

What I propose is to leave it like:

```
$CONDA_PYTHON_EXE $_CE_I -m conda $@
```

We still need to configure whether to use `-I` or not; we do use it for normal mode, but we omit it for dev mode so PYTHONPATH can be read. Right now, all the code in this PR assumes `_CE_M` and `_CE_CONDA` are always defined, but I'd say we just hardcode it because these do not need to be configurable.

It might not explicitly move, but it has strong implications. Consider `foo(std::move(bar))`. This should imply that `foo` consumes `bar`. If not, `foo` should take `bar` in by reference, pointer, or value. Except with forwarding const references in templates, where you don't use `std::move` anyway, r-value references' primary use is moving an item. `std::move` doesn't move, but it is designed to indicate something can be move; it is an explicit cast that says 'this object may be moved', otherwise it wouldn't be called `std::move`. In most contexts, `foo(std::move(x))` implies that `foo` consumes `x`, because otherwise you shouldn't invoke `std::move`. Even here in the constructor that is what we are doing, `object`'s constructor consumes the `object` portion of the instance we are passing it. Substituting `std::move` is an example, if we have `object{std::move(s)}` object is clearly consuming `s`, that is why we use `std::move`. Thus things wrapped in `std::move`, after passed to a function, should be considered 'already moved / altered'. The difference here is that with `std::forward<object>(s)` we are not saying 'consume s', but rather 'consume the object part of s'. In other words, 'the non-object part of s will not be touched by the function and we can safely use the rest of `s` after this function call'.

I think here it's important to provide details of what we're tying to avoid (an extra copy of the output), and when we should be able to remove those checks. May I suggest the following:

> There is a weird behaviour in torch core where the output tensor of `interpolate()` can be allocated as contiguous even though the input is un-ambiguously channels_last (https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/issues/68430). In particular this happens for the typical torchvision use-case of single CHW images where we fake the batch dim to become 1CHW. Below, we restride those tensors to trick torch core into properly allocating the output as channels_last, thus preserving the memory format of the input. This is not just for format consistency: for uint8 bilinear images, this also avoids an extra copy (re-packing) of the output and saves time.

There are two phases that the looping through `self._indices` can be in:
1) i is pointing to a tuple (eps_idx, ts_in_eps_idx) that's inside a non-evicted episode.
2) i is pointing to a tuple that's inside the just evicted episode.

When 1): idx_cursor is an int pointing to the first entry to keep for the new compiled `self._indices`.
When 2): idx_cursor is None
When transitioning from 1) to 2): collect all tuples from `idx_cursor` to i into the new compiled `self._indices` to make sure the non-evicted episodes will be in there.
When transitioning from 2) to 1): Do NOT anything to the new compiled `self._indices` (in order to skip the evicted episode) and set `idx_cursor` to the current i.

> Do you mean we need to check that each `seq` has exactly the same number of columns? 

exactly

> Doing that here is perfectly ok and won't incur efficiency degradation.

Actually we wouldn't know that for sure. 
To exemplify, say our user decided to implement some `seq`s with some very heavy data provider backend and somehow implemented data prefetching and parallelism as their optimization. (In extreme cases imagine data are generated on the fly from some server instances spawned upon our `touching` to the data), 

They might would trigger the generation/read for their whole `seq` when we touch the `seq` since they would expect data are accessed in ordered fashion.

To generalize, we really cannot make assumptions on how much a single access to data would cost. we **hope** they would provide best performance for both **random data access** and **by batch access**.  However in reality, these two are naturally conflicting each other in someway. Assuring batch access is in-order, at least giving an option to do so, is the best we can do to that.

L'ARE (l'allocation d'Aide au Retour à l'Emploi) semble être une composante du `chomage_brut`.

Voici quelques éléments ayant mené à cette conclusion : 
 
* **Montant brut ?**
Le montant de l'ARE est bien un montant brut si on se réfère à sa valeur journalière indiquée comme montant brut par [service-public](https://www.service-public.fr/particuliers/vosdroits/N178) qu'il s'agisse du cas d'un salarié du privé ou d'un agent public.

* **ARE composante du chomage_brut ?** 
On en a déjà parlé sur openfisca-france : c'est du moins ce qui est envisagé par ce [commentaire](https://github.com/openfisca/openfisca-france/pull/1150#pullrequestreview-162634724) de @benjello sur la PR qui supprimait la variable inutilisée `allocation_aide_retour_emploi` 👻 .

➡️ Reste à identifier la formule qui rattache l'`are` au `chomage_brut` : 

Je propose dans un premier temps de les définir comme égales (formula de `chomage_brut` qui renvoie la valeur d'`are`). Est-ce que cela vous semblerait acceptable @MattiSG @benjello ? 
L'autre option me semblerait d'y joindre l'ASS. 🤔 

Pourquoi ? : 

* **Si l'ARE n'est qu'une composante du chômage brut, quelles sont les autres composantes ?**
L'ARE est la principale allocation chômage au regard de [cette page de pôle emploi](https://www.pole-emploi.fr/candidat/mes-droits-aux-aides-et-allocati/lessentiel-a-savoir-sur-lallocat/quelle-somme-vais-je-recevoir/comment-est-calcule-le-montant-d.html).
Néanmoins, si l'on définit le `chomage_brut` comme la somme des revenus de remplacement pour les demandeurs d'emploi, alors selon les dispositions générales décrites par l'[article L5421-2 du code du travail](https://www.legifrance.gouv.fr/codes/article_lc/LEGIARTI000037388221) il en existe de plusieurs sortes que l'on retrouve dans les [chapitres dédiés aux indemnisations](https://www.legifrance.gouv.fr/codes/section_lc/LEGITEXT000006072050/LEGISCTA000006160812/#LEGISCTA000037388216) : allocation d'assurance (ARE seule ?), allocations de solidarité (ASS, allocation équivalent retraite) et allocations des régimes particuliers.

* **Salarié du privé et agent public :**
Si l'[on sait](https://www.service-public.fr/particuliers/vosdroits/N178) que l'ARE concerne les deux catégories de demandeurs d'emploi, il faudrait une analyse plus fine pour déterminer ce qu'il en est des autres types d'allocations / revenus de remplacement. Et c'est une source de complexité pour la modélisation de la formule du `chomage_brut` qui me semble dépasser le périmètre de cette PR.

---
> Mémos divers : 
> * L'[allocation spécifique de solidarité (ASS)](https://www.pole-emploi.fr/candidat/mes-droits-aux-aides-et-allocati/aides-financieres-et-autres-allo/autres-allocations/lallocation-de-solidarite-specif.html) qui prend le relais de l'ARE est aussi un [minima social](https://www.insee.fr/fr/metadonnees/definition/c1247).
> * Quid de l'[AREF](https://www.service-public.fr/particuliers/vosdroits/F291) = ARE formation ? Est-ce une allocation d'assurance au même titre que l'ARE ?
> * Les allocations [ATA et AER](https://www.service-public.fr/particuliers/actualites/A14083) (allocation équivalent retraite) ont elles été supprimées ([ici](https://travail-emploi.gouv.fr/emploi/indemnisation/article/allocation-temporaire-d-attente-ata) ce qui est arrivé à l'ATA, [ici](https://www.service-public.fr/particuliers/vosdroits/F13929) pour l'AER).

It's actually is a good pattern. Arena will not release allocated block and also it won't reuse the freed memory. 
Also to answer your other question mentioned, it'll auto increase. Internally, it's chained blocks. There is a parameter that we can tune for the size of each block.

When allocate, it'll allocate blocks and in destruction time, it'll free blocks. So protobuf won't trigger any destruction (think about complicated map objects destruction which is expensive).

In the end, all sending requests should all be built on top of the arena I think. For reply, it's not necessary and we should make replies non-const so that we can release them manually and attach them to the main thread structure.

Some references here:
>Granularity
We have found in most application server use cases that an "arena-per-request" model works well. You may be tempted to divide arena use further, either to reduce heap overhead (by destroying smaller arenas more often) or to reduce perceived thread-contention issues. However, the use of more fine-grained arenas may lead to unintended message copying, as we describe above. We have also spent effort to optimize the Arena implementation for the multithreaded use-case, so a single arena should be appropriate for use throughout a request lifetime even if multiple threads process that request.

```
from typing import Optional, Union

# For python < 3.9, else built-in tuple and collections.abc.Generator
from typing import Generator, Tuple

# Type aliases to make unpacking what's going on a little more human friendly
ContextNode = Union[BNode, URIRef]
DatasetQuad = Tuple[Node, URIRef, Node, Optional[ContextNode]]
...
def __iter__(self) -> Generator[DatasetQuad, None, None]:
...
```
I think that the alias that I suggested above for `DatasetQuad` may even be able to be made more specific. The usage of `URIRef` reflects the RDF specification that the properties of triples are all URI references. Analogous restrictions exist on the subjects (I believe that literals cannot be subjects, for example). It may be helpful to look into what the context field can be. I believe the context here can only be `None`, a `BNode`, or a `URIRef`, but it's been a little bit since I looked around.

Nit: I prefer to use `@pytest.skipif` and two separate tests. That way, when we drop old versions, we delete a whole test rather than lines in a test.

```python
@pytest.skipif(not sanic.__version__.startswith("20."), "requires sanic 20")
def test_instantiating_sanic_20(app):
    dockerflow = Dockerflow()
    assert "dockerflow.heartbeat" not in app.router.routes_names
    dockerflow.init_app(app)
    assert "dockerflow.heartbeat" in app.router.routes_names
    
@pytest.skipif(sanic.__version__.startswith("20."), "requires sanic 21 or later")
def test_instantiating(app):
    Dockerflow()
    assert ("__heartbeat__",) not in app.router.routes_all
    Dockerflow(app)
    assert ("__heartbeat__",) in app.router.routes_all
```

I see. I had simplified the summary of `always_iterable` to focus on the implementation for how it's used/recommended here. You're right that the implementation also addresses the concern of transforming a single item to an iterator over one item.

It solves the common pattern where a function accepts both a single item or an iterable of items but intends to iterate over them. For example consider [this change](https://github.com/jaraco/svg.charts/commit/eb7bcfe568e6cf10c977fda49e95f179e29bc7b7#diff-b5ee5757c7acd54889983e45eeb5701e1ada91dc4c236811c8a52c1cbe23c045L17-L33), where `always_iterable` transforms the branchy logic into a simple dict comprehension.

> That extra non-iterable condition is not expressible in mypy. 

That seems like a limitation of mypy. It seems to me that it should be possible to express any valid Python in mypy, especially something as simple as accepting an iterable or non-iterable. Is it possible the level of expressiveness you're trying to achieve is too great?  I notice that `always_iterable` does already have a [type specification](https://github.com/more-itertools/more-itertools/blob/31b8a0ed1c8a60d7c6b96bc6d2b7c64c646a1057/more_itertools/more.pyi#L285-L290). I wighted between named tuple and attribute based class.
After a conversation with @jaeopt I opted for the class because it allows better extensibility. it's easy to add a new attribute to OptmizelyDecisionContext should we want to do that in the future.
Writing a class with __eq__ and __hash_- methods is the same as using a named tuple but it offers extensibility.

I think this creates an unexpected case where `flags` is passed in but is overridden by the serializer. I can think of two improvements:

1. Because `flags` is a bit field, we could `OR` the two values together (the `flags` argument plus these `serializer_flags`) to form the final value send to the server. I don't know if that would always be the right thing to do, however, because we can't know in advance how the server and serializer are going to use the flags.
2. We could raise an exception when both `flags` and `self.serializer` are provided because they're incompatible.

@annawoodard %#x formats `id(self)` as hex, which fits in with how other objects are __repr_-ed; for example:

```
>>> x = map(str,[1,2,3])
>>> x
<map object at 0x7f69ff01aac8>
>>> id(x)
140093226592968
>>> hex(id(x))
'0x7f69ff01aac8'
```

The data here can actually come in as any string. It's up to the user to decide the contents of the message. We support two cases:
- json string
- base64 encoded json string

If it's neither of these, then we do not inject the trace header into the message. I'll add a comment about this in the code.

Yeah sorry the `i` and `j` indexing is a lot to keep straight. You're probably are already aware that `i` is the iteration over the components of `eval_set` that are, at a minimum, to be comprised of the tuple `(X_i, y_i)`. And then `j` is the index of the iteration along the parts of one `(X_i, y_i)` along with possibly all of the extra distributed vectors like `eval_init_score[i]`.

I don't believe there is any overwriting going on - the logic is append-only. So when we go from `i=0` to `i=1` we get

```
i = 1

eval_sets = {
    0: [([x_e0_0, x_e0_3, x_e0_6], [y_e0_0, y_e0_3, y_e0_6]), ([x_e1_0], [y_e1_0])],
    1: [([x_e0_1, x_e0_4, None],         [y_e0_1, y_e0_4, None]),         ([None], [None])],
    2: [([x_e0_2, x_e0_5, None],         [y_e0_2, y_e0_5, None]),         ([None], [None])],
}
```

This is because of lines 530 and 531 that initiate a new tuple for the next component of `eval_set` (i.e. `i=1`):
```
if j < n_parts:
    eval_sets[parts_idx].append(([x_e], [y_e]))
```
So when `i` changes from 0 to 1 and then we re-enter the inner loop over `j`, then since `j=0, ...`, the `if j < n_parts:` condition is always reached first, and we append a new tuple, `([x_e1_0, y_e1_0])`. Then on line 531, once we've hit the round-robin criteria, we start appending `X_ij`'s to the first component of the last tuple contained in `eval_sets[parts_idx]` (append-only).
